Federal Minister Jitendra Singh denied Gandhi's allegation, external - he said he informed the Congress MP that the government had accepted his demand for a discussion but alleged that Gandhi later also demanded the resignation of Pradhan. He accused Gandhi of "changing the goalpost" and of refusing to end the protest despite being told that the place wasn't conducive to such demonstrations. Later, Pradhan - in his first comments since Monday's violence - criticised the Congress for "shamelessly exploiting students as political tools". "Even after the government conveyed its readiness for a comprehensive discussion, the Congress chose political spectacle over democratic debate. Their objective was never solutions for students, it was disruption for political headlines," he said in a post on X. Pradhan did not directly refer to the CJP or the protests but said: "We owe our students more than outrage. We owe them answers, reforms and accountability. That is exactly what our government remains committed to delivering." Pradhan's resignation has been one of the key demands of the protesters and the CJP has not responded to his comments so far. A satirical political movement, CJP was inspired by remarks made earlier this year by India's chief justice, who used the word "cockroach" to describe unemployed young people turning to journalism and activism. After the comments sparked outrage, he said he had been referring only to those with "fake and bogus degrees". Monday's huge turnout showed that the CJP, though not a registered political party, has evolved into a movement capable of drawing tens of thousands onto the streets. Protesters, who have been camped for the past month at Jantar Mantar, gained global attention after activist and educationist Sonam Wangchuk joined them on 28 June and began an indefinite hunger strike in support of their demands. He was forcibly removed from the protest site by the police on Saturday and taken to hospital where he continues to fast. The movement has broadened into one of the most visible expressions of public dissent against Modi in recent years. Over the past few days, support for the CJP has grown beyond Delhi, with protests also being held in other major cities like Mumbai, Hyderabad, Bengaluru and Ahmedabad.

item.title: Police station opens at Hitler's birthplace in bid to rid site of Nazi link
item.description:
【原文链接】：https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/cy5d36e752yo?at_medium=RSS&at_campaign=rss
========================================
Police station opens at Hitler's birthplace in bid to rid site of Nazi link - Published Austria has opened a police station in the house where Adolf Hitler was born in the town of Braunau am Inn. It follows years of controversy about what to do with the 17th-Century former inn, where Nazi dictator Adolf Hitler was born in April 1889. The building has now been substantially refurbished and its facade changed, but it has been a huge headache for Austrian authorities, who want to prevent it from becoming a site of neo-Nazi pilgrimage. Hitler only lived in Salzburger Vorstadt 15 for a few weeks before his family moved to another address in Braunau. They left the town for good when he was three years old. Hitler returned briefly to Braunau in 1938, on his way to Vienna, after he annexed Austria to Nazi Germany. The opening ceremony for the police station, featuring police musicians playing a tuba and other brass instruments, was attended by Interior Minister Gerhard Karner and other local dignitaries. Austria was taking "responsibility" for the "terrible legacy" of the past, said Karner. While deciding what to do with the building had not been easy, Karner believed the police station marked "a new chapter" for it. "This house will now become a place of democracy, rule of law, security, freedom and human dignity," he said. A Catholic police chaplain then blessed two crosses which will hang in the new police station. For decades, the interior ministry had rented the building from its former owner, a local woman called Gerlinde Pommer. It paid her a generous rent, in an attempt to prevent the house being used as a site for extreme right-wing tourism. The building was used for many years by a local charity as a day centre and workshop for people with special needs. But it was forced to move out in 2011, when Pommer blocked renovations. She also repeatedly refused to sell the property to the government. The house remained empty for several years, while the authorities and Pommer argued over terms. In 2016, Austria's parliament passed a law allowing the government to seize the house from Pommer. It paid her compensation of €812,000 (£690,000). She later took the government to court, saying the sum was too low, but lost her case. The interior minister at the time, Wolfgang Sobotka, said the building should be torn down and a new one erected in its place. It was an echo of an earlier offer by a Russian MP who said he wanted to buy the house and blow it up. But there was a storm of criticism, with many saying that destroying the house would be tantamount to denying Austria's Nazi past. In 2019, the government announced plans to turn it into a police station and initiated an EU-wide competition for architects to submit designs. Millions of euros were spent refurbishing the old yellow building, which now looks very different from before. Now painted white, inside and out, the building is full of gleaming oak woodwork. An old winding staircase and arched entrance hall have been preserved. It looks very different from when the Lebenshilfe charity was running a small shop in one of the rooms at the front, when I last went inside almost 20 years ago. Police Chief Inspector Ludwig Heise said he was happy to have such "a great, modern building" to work in. "We have more space," he said, "but otherwise it is like any other police station in Austria." Not everyone is happy with the decision to open a police station there, with some arguing it sends the wrong signal. Some had wanted the house to become a centre of responsibility, confronting the Nazi past. Others, like local historian Florian Kotanko, say they would have liked to have seen the Lebenshilfe charity return. "But even this charity organisation did not want to come back because they thought they would be used as a fig leaf for a political discussion, and so the government had to decide in another way," Kotanko told the BBC. There is no sign on the house about its history and the building features security cameras to record the activities of any potential neo-Nazi pilgrims. All that links it to Hitler is a stone on the street outside, which was erected in 1989. It says: "Never again Fascism; In memory of millions of dead." The stone comes from the nearby former concentration camp at Mauthausen. Hitler's name does not appear. However, Florian Kotanko believes the site's history will never go away even if fewer neo-Nazis pay a visit. "You have to deal with it, and so we all try to deal with it in an open and correct way. It's not a matter of being proud, but it's a matter of dealing with the fact."

item.title: Trump's nuclear deal with Saudis jettisons longstanding US demands
item.description:
【原文链接】：https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/cz05lx0xpero?at_medium=RSS&at_campaign=rss
========================================
Trump's nuclear deal with Saudis jettisons longstanding US demands - Published The Trump administration's nuclear agreement with Saudi Arabia reversed a years-long stance toward a key Middle East ally. Past US presidents insisted that Saudi Arabia normalise relations with Israel in exchange for receiving American nuclear technology for civilian energy programmes. According to reports of the deal's contents, President Donald Trump gave Saudi Arabia what it wanted without extracting concessions for Israel in return - and over objections from critics who argued that it could spark a nuclear arms race in the region. On Thursday he said in a social media post that the nuclear pact was conditioned on Saudi Arabia joining the Abraham Accords, a series of deals reached in his first term that normalised relations between Israel and several other Gulf states. But it was not immediately clear if that was stipulated in the text of the deal - which has not been released - or that Saudi Arabia had agreed to that condition, or would be forced to join the accords. The deal appears to be the latest example of Trump jettisoning the traditional American approach to the Middle East, which centred on Israel and finding a two-state solution to the Israeli-Palestinian conflict. In his second term Trump has focused primarily on strengthening America's economic influence to compete with China and other rivals. Analysts said the nuclear pact with Saudi Arabia sent a signal that the US wants to keep regional allies in the fold even if it means angering Israel in the process. "Countries in the Middle East want to develop their nuclear infrastructure and they're going to do it even if the US is not involved. Washington at this point is recognising that," said Yusuf Can, a Middle East expert at consulting firm Amena Strategies. With this nuclear deal, he added, "the US is basically saying you don't have to work with China. You can work with us". - US signs landmark nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia - Published4 hours ago
- Tankers make sharp U-turns after Houthi shipping threat - Published23 hours ago
Transactional foreign policy has been a hallmark of Trump's second term. The US has ratcheted up economic competition with adversaries like China and Russia in the Arctic and other parts of the world since Trump returned to office vowing to revive his "America First" approach to global affairs. But signing a nuclear pact in the Middle East is an especially bold move that will raise tensions in a volatile region where the US is at war with Iran. It represents a major victory for Saudi Arabia's Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman, who has sought a nuclear agreement with the US for years as part of a broader campaign to strengthen relations with the West. The crown prince, known as MBS, said in 2018 that if Iran develops nuclear weapons then Saudi Arabia should be allowed to as well. Details of the deal were not immediately released by the administration, but the pact reportedly does not include strict enforcement measures to block Saudi Arabia from developing a military nuclear programme in the future. In that respect it differs from a 2009 nuclear cooperation agreement struck by the US and the United Arab Emirates which included strict inspections by the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA). Another key difference that critics of the Saudi deal are pointing to is that it reportedly allows them to enrich uranium themselves on their own soil. Even if Saudi Arabia doesn't race to develop nuclear weapons right away, the potential ability to do so could still give the country a powerful deterrent force in the region, analysts said. "It certainly helps lay the groundwork for nuclear weapons in the future," said Imran Bayoumi, a resident fellow at the Atlantic Council think tank and former policy adviser at the US Defence Department. The agreement will allow Saudi Arabia to diversify its energy sector and use nuclear power to meet rising electricity demands, energy experts said. Having nuclear power could eventually lessen Saudi Arabia's reliance on oil, insulating it from volatilities in the global oil market, said Christopher Russo, an energy expert at the consulting firm Charles River Associates. "Oil can be disrupted. You can bomb oil fields. Oil tankers can be stuck somewhere, as we're seeing right now" in the Strait of Hormuz, Russo said. "Nuclear power is pretty self-contained." Saudi Arabia won't reap the economic rewards anytime soon. It will likely take a decade if not longer for the country to build and open new nuclear power plants. But the short-term political payoff is clear, as Saudi Arabia - already one of the most powerful states in the Gulf - further cements its status as a major force in the region. Moreover, Saudi Arabia struck the deal without having to recognise Israel or drop its demand that an eventual normalisation deal with Israel be tied to the creation of a Palestinian state. That concession appeared to reflect a calculus by the Trump administration that a diplomatic breakthrough between Saudi Arabia and Israel isn't likely to happen for some time. The countries were open to talks during the Biden administration, but negotiations stalled after the Israel-Hamas conflict in Gaza and the US-Israel war with Iran. The timing for the administration's announcement isn't clearly explained. Officials did not say why the US has chosen to enter this agreement with Saudi Arabia now. But the war with Iran likely played a part. Washington has sought to ease tensions with Saudi Arabia and other Gulf allies who have been targeted by Tehran since the start of the conflict. Saudi Arabia briefly denied the US access to Saudi airspace earlier in the war, and has pushed the US and Iran to de-escalate. American companies will likewise benefit from the nuclear deal, though that also won't happen overnight. Saudi Arabia will work with US-based firms to develop its civilian nuclear technology, opening a lucrative market for companies like Westinghouse that manufacture large-scale conventional nuclear reactors. - Why and how is US blockading Iranian ports in Strait of Hormuz? - Published30 April
- 'What was their crime?': BBC visits Iran school where strike killed 120 children - Published8 hours ago
Nevertheless the deal is already facing criticism in Israel and the US. As news of the deal emerged on Wednesday, some Israeli politicians argued Saudi Arabia could become a new nuclear threat in the region. Israel is widely believed to have nuclear weapons, though it has not acknowledged so publicly, and it joined the US war with Iran over shared concerns about Tehran's nuclear programme. "A civilian nuclear program in Saudi Arabia [will] lead to a mad arms race across the entire Middle East," Avigdor Lieberman, a member of the Israeli parliament and a former defense minister, said in a social media post. Democratic lawmakers in Congress raised similar concerns. "The deal really is a giveaway to the Saudis, and it's only going to fuel further ambitions from Iran. It's only going to further destabilise the region with Israel," Democratic congressman Ro Khanna of California told the BBC. "It's just not smart policy." US Energy Secretary Chris Wright said the agreement would boost the American economy and strengthen national security. Wright also said in a statement posted to social media that the US-Saudi pact would "uphold the highest standards of nuclear safety and nonproliferation." But the administration fuelled scepticism over the agreement by not immediately releasing more information about it on Wednesday. And perhaps in a telling sign, Trump did not preside over the signing ceremony making the deal official. Trump attended the dignified transfer ceremony for four US service members who were killed in Iran, before holding a rally in Georgia where he argued that his policies were making the Middle East more stable than ever.

item.title: 'What was their crime?': BBC visits Iran school where strike killed 120 children
item.description:
【原文链接】：https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/cewrx0ldlkko?at_medium=RSS&at_campaign=rss
========================================
'What was their crime?': BBC visits Iran school where strike killed 120 children - Published This report contains distressing descriptions of the deaths of children. Amaineh Nademi walks carefully through what is left of her old school. Chunks of broken concrete hang precariously from the damaged upper floor. A few classrooms are still standing, their brightly coloured walls decorated with painted balloons and flowers. The 33-year-old teacher knows exactly what was where - the girls' classrooms on the upper floor, the boys' classrooms below, the headteacher's office, the prayer room. Rubble is all that remains of her classroom, where she taught first-grade girls at Shajareh Tayyebeh Primary School in Minab, a small city in southern Iran. On 28 February, in the opening hours of the US-Israeli war with Iran, a strike on the school transformed an ordinary Saturday morning into one of the conflict's defining tragedies - and its deadliest single incident for civilians. In total, 156 people, including 120 children, have been confirmed killed, including a pregnant woman and her unborn child, according to the Minab prosecutor's office. Experts say evidence indicates that US forces hit the school, which is next to an Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) compound that satellite images show was struck several times. The US has not admitted responsibility, though US media have reported that military investigators believe it is likely that US forces carried out the strike unintentionally. The pain of what happened here is still raw. Teachers tell us they pray at the site every day. Rescue workers are still searching for remains of those killed here. Much of the upper floor collapsed into the one below. "You can't call it a school," says Amaineh – just "walls that smell of death, grief and the blood that was shed here". The Iranian authorities were keen for us to visit the school – it is evidence, they say, that a campaign presented by Washington as an attack on Iran's military and security apparatus has instead inflicted a devastating cost on civilians. 'We were all sent flying' The school day had begun as usual, Amaineh says. Because it was the Islamic holy month of Ramadan, the children gathered inside the prayer hall to recite the Quran, she recalls, before she began teaching her pupils a new letter of the Persian alphabet. But, as the first attacks of the war unfolded across Iran, she says the headteacher announced the school would be closing, and teachers began contacting parents, urging them to collect their children. Only five of Amaineh's 15 first-grade pupils were left in her classroom on the upper floor, she says, when she first heard an explosion not far from the school. She says she was in the corridor and rushed back for the children. "Just as I went to leave, a missile struck the school," says Amaineh. "Something hit my head and my students' heads - we were all sent flying." Then, she says, thick black smoke filled the classroom: "I couldn't even see the pupils I was holding in my arms. We couldn't breathe." She says she remembers hearing another explosion, as well as the sounds of screaming, children crying and then the school itself collapsing – "destroyed all at once". "Blood was pouring from me and the students," she adds. When the smoke briefly cleared, she says she passed the injured children down to people below and clambered past fire and rubble towards the school gate, where she saw a crowd of parents. Many had rushed to the site without even putting shoes on, she recalls. She says she could not understand why the parents were running towards the school, believing in her shock and confusion that everyone else had already escaped the building: "I didn't know that all of them were under the rubble." Amaineh had taught first grade at the school for six years. She lists the names of students and colleagues killed that day: Adrina from her first grade class, who she says was killed on the stairs with her mother, Fatemeh in second grade, and many others including students in sixth grade she remembers teaching the alphabet to, as well as the headteacher, deputy head, PE teacher and Quran teacher. 'Find my son' Within minutes, emergency workers began arriving. Reza Besharati, a worker with the Iranian Red Crescent Society, says he was among the first rescuers on the scene, and found the roads near the school filled with traffic as desperate families rushed to look for their children. Minab is a small city. As Reza made his way through the crowd and into the wreckage, he says he recognised people he knew, including some of his own relatives. "We started to find the people, thinking maybe someone would be alive," he says. "But unfortunately, nobody was." He says the dead were found grimly disfigured: "Maybe just a hand, maybe just a leg… Nobody had a complete body." Many families recognised their children by their shoes or clothing, he adds. One of Reza's relatives was searching and begging "just find my child, find my son", he says. "I couldn't tell her that her son was not alive. We were all just crying." Reza says the sights he saw while searching the collapsed building have not left him: "I couldn't sleep for a few days," he says. "When I close my eyes… I just remember these things that I have seen here." Strike investigated As details of the strike emerged, President Donald Trump first blamed Iran, without providing evidence. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said the military was investigating and that US forces "never target civilian targets". He later announced a higher-level investigation, led by an officer from outside US Central Command (Centcom). The Trump administration has still not released the findings. Several US media outlets have reported - citing people familiar with the US military investigation - that preliminary findings concluded it was likely that outdated data supplied by the US Defence Intelligence Agency led to the school being treated as part of a military site. Expert video analysis has shown the IRGC complex next to the school was hit by a precision guided Tomahawk missile - a type of cruise missile that neither Israel nor Iran is known to possess. Satellite imagery showed the school and several buildings in the compound had been hit, and verified videos showed the area was hit by a series of strikes. US military maps also confirm American operations in southern Iran – including the Minab area - at the time. Historical satellite images show the school area was previously part of the IRGC complex, but has been walled off since 2016 and has its own entrances and yards. Also, information indicating the site was a school was publicly available online. Archived snapshots of the websites for the girls' and boys' sections of the school – taken in the months before the attack - include the address of the site. The head of Centcom told a congressional committee in May, without giving further detail, that the investigation was "complex" as the school was "on an active IRGC cruise missile base" – which Iran's foreign ministry rejected as a "baseless fabrication". The IRGC runs some community and medical facilities in addition to its extensive military, security and economic activities. Local residents have told BBC Persian that the site had previously been a base for an IRGC naval brigade, but that they believed its military use had ceased several years ago. Photographs show buildings on the compound included a clinic and a car wash. The BBC approached the Department of Defense and the US military for comment. Officials said the investigation was ongoing and there were no further updates. 'Identified by her socks' A short drive from the ruined school is the cemetery where many of the pupils and teachers are buried. Every evening, families gather beside the graves. Mothers wipe dust from photographs of their children. Parents sit and pray long after darkness has fallen. Around them, brothers and sisters play between the headstones of siblings whose lives were cut short. Masoumeh Mehran Shekhabadi comes to visit her nine-year-old daughter, Setayesh, every day, often staying long into the night. She was among the parents who ran to the school after the strike. "There was no school," she says, her voice breaking. "There was only smoke, children's hair, a hand on one side, a foot on the other. They had been torn to pieces." Masoumeh adds: "I kept calling out, not only to Setayesh, but to all the children: 'Children, where are you?'" Setayesh's family did not receive confirmation of her death until the early hours of the following morning, Masoumeh says. She remembers being repeatedly asked what colour socks the child had been wearing, and replying that they were cream. Setayesh was then identified by her socks, she says. The nine-year-old had wanted to be a teacher, her mother adds. "She would line up her dolls on the couch and say: 'I am your teacher. I'm in primary school now, so I am a teacher.'" As for many parents here, grief has become intertwined with anger. "May Trump be destroyed, so these children's blood is not wasted," Masoumeh says. 'They'd gone to learn' Iran's government has repeatedly invoked Minab in speeches, international forums and state media coverage - even naming a plane carrying negotiators "Minab-168", in reference to the death toll previously given by officials. The attack offers the Islamic Republic a powerful counter-narrative to Washington's description of its campaign. The war has divided Iranians. Some celebrated when the air strikes began, angered by the government's history of detaining dissidents and using lethal force during repeated waves of protest. They hoped military pressure from outside might finally weaken or even bring down the regime. Some opponents of the government have told the BBC they saw the strike at Minab as a huge blow. It made the idea of a clean, surgical war impossible to sustain and allowed the authorities to present themselves as the defenders of the Iranian people against foreign attack. In Minab, Amaineh visits the cemetery, walking among the graves of her former pupils. On a nearby memorial wall are the faces of the children, staff and parents who left home that Saturday morning and never returned. Setayesh's mother still sits beside the grave of her little girl. "What was these children's crime? Did they carry weapons? A pencil, a book, a notebook and a schoolbag. They had no weapons… They'd gone to learn," she says. Additional reporting by Jasmin Dyer Nawal Al-Maghafi is reporting from Iran on condition that none of her material is used on the BBC's Persian Service. These restrictions apply to all international media organisations operating in Iran.

item.title: Indian police cracked down on 'cockroach' protesters. They went home and made memes about it
item.description:
【原文链接】：https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/c3ek3l9gp7go?at_medium=RSS&at_campaign=rss
========================================
Indian police cracked down on 'cockroach' protesters. They went home and made memes about it - Published On Monday, 20-year-old Chhavi (name changed) paused outside a Delhi metro station on her way to what would become one of the largest demonstrations against Prime Minister Narendra Modi's government in the Indian capital in recent years. She balanced her phone against a railing, stepped back and filmed a quick "fit check" - a video showing off her outfit before the protest. "Going to get beaten by cops, guys," she smiled into the camera. Hours later, police fired tear gas and charged demonstrators with batons as tens of thousands of supporters of the Cockroach Janta Party (CJP) – a youth-led movement that grew from protests over exam irregularities into a broader campaign for government accountability – marched towards parliament. They demanded the resignation of Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan over exam paper leaks and recruitment scandals. Pradhan has refused to resign, accusing the opposition of exploiting students for political gain. Authorities suspended mobile internet around the protest site. By evening, protesters said scores had been beaten by police, while police said a number of officers had also been injured. When Chhavi finally uploaded the videos that evening, one thing stood out. Before the march, she joked about being beaten by police. Hours later, she was still smiling - posing beside riot officers, flashing victory signs, filming through crowds and even interviewing an injured protester. The clips were among thousands of reels, memes and first-person dispatches that flooded Instagram once protesters regained internet access. Some were raw with anger. Others were breathless, frightened or quietly defiant. Yet it was often the sardonic humour that drew the eye. One young woman calmly reapplied her lipstick in front of a line of riot police. Another joked she was "serving face at the protest because the government couldn't serve justice". One reel began as a routine outfit reveal before abruptly cutting to protesters fleeing tear gas. Another gave a deadpan "review" of tear gas, rating its smell and sting like a new restaurant. To many watching online, the humour seemed strangely at odds with the violence. For the young people making the videos, there was no contradiction. None of the protesters interviewed for this story spoke about algorithms, engagement or going viral. They described filming as almost inseparable from protesting itself. And when they turned to humour, it was almost always self-directed: joking about the kilometres logged fleeing baton charges or the absurdity of filming an outfit reveal moments before running into tear gas. The jokes did not erase the fear. They coexisted with it. Experts say the trend hints at a broader shift in how a generation raised online experiences protest. "Older generations separated political life from everyday life," said Joyojeet Pal, a professor at the University of Michigan who studies technology and society. "Younger generations grow up with politics alongside their memes, their family pictures and their daily commutes." A protest, in other words, becomes just another moment to record. Chhavi continued to film even after the crackdown began. Some commenters later accused her of treating the violence as content. "But I was adrenaline-soaked, scared, petrified," she said. "It was either laugh in the face of fear or cry." That instinct to keep filming, even as the situation turned violent, is one that Payal Arora, a digital anthropologist at Utrecht University, says has become increasingly common around the world. Smartphones have turned ordinary people into chroniclers of political upheaval, recording moments that might otherwise disappear. "The act of capturing a protest is often an end in itself," she said. "Their message is more like: 'This happened. We were there. We will remember.'" The humour, too, followed a pattern. No young protester described the day as funny. They spoke instead of tear gas, baton charges and the bewilderment of watching a peaceful march descend into chaos. The jokes came later, almost instinctively, as a way of carrying that experience. - India's 'cockroach' protest continues a day after police crackdown - Published2 days ago
- India has a new political superstar - a cockroach - Published21 May
- Why are thousands protesting for education reform in India? - Published3 hours ago
- The hunger-striking engineer demanding education reforms in India - Published3 days ago
People often mistake that humour for detachment, says Nishant Shah, director of Digital Narratives Center at School of Journalism & Communication at the Chinese University of Hong Kong. In reality, he argues, it serves almost the opposite purpose. "Politics is everyday, emotional and experiential," he said. Humour has long been part of protest movements. It can create solidarity, ease tension and help people endure moments of fear. "Even when the cause is brutal and the fight fierce," Shah said, "there is joy, laughter, songs and hope. Without those things, protests don't sustain themselves." Ishaan (name changed), 21, came to Delhi for his first protest. When tear gas shells landed nearby, he briefly considered leaving. Instead, he reached for his phone. The reel he posted later showed white smoke drifting across the street beneath a simple caption: "Delhi Police doesn't realise I'm from Jamuna Paar." For most Delhiites, the joke needed little explanation. Jamuna Paar - literally "across the Yamuna" - is shorthand for the city's eastern neighbourhoods and the subject of endless teasing over their neglected civic infrastructure. "The smell there is worse than the tear gas," Ishan said, laughing. He told the BBC the joke was never about pretending nothing had happened. "It was about refusing to let fear dictate the memory of the day," he said. Protests have always found ways to hold fear and hope in the same frame. India's freedom movement had songs and speeches. Trade unions marched behind banners. Student movements embraced posters and street theatre. More recently, the anti-citizenship protests were marked by murals and public readings of the constitution, while the farmers' movement became synonymous with tractor rallies and livestreams from Delhi's borders. Like many CJP protesters, Ishan spoke in the idiom of a generation raised online. To dismiss that language as frivolous, Shah argues, is to misunderstand what it is doing. "We often make the mistake of trying to understand new technologies through older ways of thinking," Shah said. But a meme is not simply today's version of a political poster, he said. It gathers meaning as people remake it, parody it and pass it on. Sharing it becomes another way of participating in the protest. That was true for Manit, an 18-year-old engineering student. It was his first protest, and he had almost decided not to film. "I was scared," he said. Then he looked around. "Everyone was recording, talking to the camera, just being themselves. So I asked my friend to record me." At first glance, the reel resembled the workout videos he usually posted. Only the final frame revealed the distance on his fitness app had been logged while fleeing police baton charges. "I escaped the police," he laughed. "I ran and ran and ended up completing my workout too." For Manit, that irony was precisely the point. "Not all young people read the news," he said. "If humour can make important conversations accessible, why wouldn't we use it?" *Some people's names have been changed to protect their identity Follow BBC News India on Instagram, external, YouTube,, external X, external and Facebook, external.

item.title: A year after deadly jet crash at Bangladesh school, families demand answers
item.description:
【原文链接】：https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/cx2j7jgg1z1o?at_medium=RSS&at_campaign=rss
========================================
A year after deadly jet crash at Bangladesh school, families demand answers - Published It was a normal day for sixth-grade student Nure Jannat Yusha when suddenly, a Bangladeshi air force jet crashed into her school in the capital, Dhaka, killing 36 people - most of them children. Within minutes, Milestone School and College, a private institution buzzing with student activity, was transformed into a scene of chaos and raging flames. It was the deadliest aviation disaster the country had seen in decades. Earlier this week, families and survivors gathered to mark one year since the incident - many of them students who say they are struggling to return to normal life, Yusha among them. "Even now, if I go to school and see a plane, I feel afraid. I can't sleep at night. In my dreams, someone is throwing me into the fire," she told BBC Bengali. She was one of more than 160 people who were injured in the crash at Milestone School - and among those at the memorial held at a hospital in Dhaka on Tuesday, where the families of some victims say they are still waiting for answers about the deadly crash. Yusha's mother Yasin Akter says her child had once dreamt of becoming a dancer - an aspiration that has since faded. "My daughter screams all day and is afraid," she says, adding that Yusha has become paranoid about planes falling from the sky. "Earlier she was interested in studies. Now she does not want to study any more." 'No one can guarantee another plane will not fall' On 21 July 2025, a training aircraft took off from a Bangladesh Air Force Base in Dhaka at around 13:00 local time (07:00 GMT) and crashed soon after in the Uttara neighbourhood. Images from the scene hours after the crash showed scores of emergency service workers sorting through charred rubble in an attempt to find survivors. Authorities announced that an investigation commission had been formed to investigate the crash, but in the days that followed, hundreds of student protesters took to the streets, clashing with police as they demanded accountability over the disaster and changes to training flights regulations. The committee's findings, which it reportedly submitted to the government last November, have not yet been made public. According to a copy of the full report obtained by BBC Bengali, it highlighted issues like Milestone School's lack of compliance with building codes. It also found more than 600 buildings - including schools and hospitals - located near Dhaka's international airport that had violated height restrictions. The report also found that the pilot lacked sufficient flight training and proper ground supervision. And there was no technical fault with the aircraft, the report said - contradicting an early statement by the Bangladeshi Armed Forces that the jet had experienced a mechanical fault after taking off. The Bangladesh Armed Forces did not respond to a request for comment. "No one can give a guarantee that another plane will not fall onto this school in the future," Mansur Helal, the husband of one victim, told the crowd during the memorial, referencing a recent BBC documentary on the committee report. "This is truly a horrific situation. Every day around 2,500-3,000 students go to that school." Helal's wife, schoolteacher Mahreen Chowdhury, had died in hospital from extensive burns she had suffered while rescuing students. "The building in which this accident happened had only one door. How can there be only one door for 800-900 students?" Helal added. Helal and others are calling for the government to release the full report. They are also urging better government support for survivors undergoing treatment, as well as compensation for them and victims' families - which was recommended in the investigative committee report but yet to be implemented. Health Minister Sardar Sakhawat Hossain, who was at the event on Tuesday, said the government would support survivors who require further medical treatment. And Zubaida Rahman, the wife of Prime Minister Tarique Rahman, hailed the courage shown by survivors as "a source of inspiration for the nation". But many of the students who attended the memorial this week, sporting burn scars, are still grappling with their new reality. Mehreen, who's in the fifth grade, says she is no longer able to go to school because of her injuries. "If I get a little sunlight, my whole body burns. For that reason, I do not go to school any more." It has also affected her sleep and learning ability: "Earlier, if I read something, I would memorise it in two minutes. Now I can't memorise a line in two hours. "I can't sleep at night. When I do sleep, I often have strange dreams. I still can't forget that incident." Related topics - Published2 days ago
- Published26 July 2025

item.title: Even camels can't cope: Africa's ships of the desert hit by rising temperatures
item.description:
【原文链接】：https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/cqlxvvw06pqo?at_medium=RSS&at_campaign=rss
========================================
Even camels can't cope: Africa's ships of the desert hit by rising temperatures - Published Camels are famously known as the ships of the desert, but even these hardy animals are not being spared from the extremes of rising temperatures in Africa. The Afar region of north-eastern Ethiopia is one of the hottest and driest places on Earth, where nomads often lead camel caravans carrying salt through the desert. "I can see my camels suffering from extreme heat," Ali Umer, a camel herder from Semera in Afar, told the BBC. "They get blisters on their feet, their eyes become watery and the hot sand burns their skin and eats up their hair when they are sitting," said the 40-year-old who owns around 500 camels. "Within the past one month I have lost eight camel calves because of the extreme heat, it has become much more intense and difficult. "Even the wind, which used to bring cool air, now carries heat," he said, adding he had noticed these drastic changes in the temperature in recent years. He is not alone in his observations: scientists, animal welfare workers and various studies all mention stress, fatigue, dehydration, disease prevalence as direct impacts of extreme heat. The higher temperatures also indirectly make camels suffer by causing severe loss of water, vegetation, and reduced shaded areas. North Africa and the Horn of Africa host around 80% of the world's largest population of dromedary camels - those with one hump. Over the years, farmers and cattle herders in these regions have switched to these animals because of their resilience to harsh heat and drought conditions. But a study based on the perception of camel herders in southern Ethiopia, published in 2025, , externalshowed that high temperatures and climate variability had had a detrimental impact on camel herd health. "Respondents reported that climate change and variability have affected camels' health, productivity, and reproductive performance," it said. In neighbouring Somalia, the situation appears to be more serious. Research published in June, external has shown that drought-related camel mortality is a widespread crisis, affecting nearly 46% of camel-owning households. The study included nearly 6,000 pastoral households and showed that the mortality was concentrated in northern regions, with Sool reporting a "staggering mortality rate of nearly 73%". Extreme heat is the main cause behind the drought in the region although other factors like inadequate veterinary services and animal management make things worse, experts say. An analysis by atmospheric scientists, external found that high temperatures in the region were behind the 2021-22 drought, as extreme heat makes water from soil and water bodies turn into vapour and plants release water in them into air as vapour. Veterinarians say they are treating more sick camels and are seeing more deaths due to extreme heat and drought. "We are seeing significant increase in cases of hyperthermia in camels as a result of which they get multiple infections," Hassan Nuya Guyo, a veterinary officer in Marsabit county in north-eastern Kenya bordering Ethiopia, told the BBC. "In the last two years, we have seen a more than 15% increase in camel deaths in our county due to extreme heat." Disease prevalence among camels and reduced milk yield from them are as a result of climate change impacts including heat stress, according to another study conducted in the Horn of Africa, external. A similar story is playing out in North Africa. A study in Algeria, external, published this year, has shown a significant increase in camel deaths during summer heatwaves. "Time series analysis highlights significant seasonal mortality patterns, underscoring the need for targeted interventions during high-risk periods like peak summer months; such as strategic water provisioning, supplemental feeding, and enhanced disease monitoring," it said. The World Meteorological Organization (WMO) says extreme heat events have become a common phenomenon in several North African countries with heatwaves having increased with a clear upward trend since 1981. It said a number of countries in the region and the Middle East reported temperatures above 50C in 2024. WMO's State of the Climate in Africa 2025 report, external said North Africa recorded the most rapid warming of the six subregions - 0.43C per decade - between 1991 and 2025. Experts say camels comfortably beat the heat up to around 40C by mainly allowing their body temperature to fluctuate by several degrees throughout the day to deal with outside temperature. By doing that they avoid sweating and, combined with concentrated urine and dry faeces, it helps them minimise water loss, while their thick skin coat helps them ward off solar radiation. "But camels can also experience heat stress when temperatures rise beyond their comfort range," said Dr Mohammed Bengoumi, a scientist with the UN's Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO), who specialises in camel health and works in North Africa. "In many parts of the Sahara, summer temperatures now frequently exceed 50C, increasing the risk of severe heat stress, particularly when accompanied by reduced vegetation cover, water scarcity, and limited access to shade," he told the BBC. Professor Abdelmadjid Chehma at the University of Ghardaia in Algeria, who has conducted experimental works on camels in the Sahara Desert, has a similar conclusion. "Our works show that prolonged day-and-night heat above 41 to 45C directly attack their cells," he told the BBC. "Their immune system suffers because such heat levels damage their white blood cells and other cells because of stress, they [the cells] struggle to repair themselves and end up destroying themselves." For camels this means when heat stress sets in, their metabolism slows down to limit internal heat production leading to sharp reduction in food intake resulting in lethargy and generalised fatigue. That leads to drop in milk production and also causes reproductive disorders. Professor Mohammed el Khasmi, animal hydrobiology expert at the University of Casablanca in Morocco, has a long list of effects of heat stress he and his team have observed on camels. Weight loss, inflammation, muscle and bone related illnesses, chemical imbalance due to kidney malfunction, infectious diseases caused by parasites were a few among them, he said. While extreme heat is the primary culprit, its impacts become severe when other factors like loss of vegetation and water, lack of shaded areas and inadequate veterinary services add up, experts say. This is why many herders in Ethiopia have moved their camels from the northern region, where the animals were found historically, to the south where water and vegetation are relatively available, experts there say. "And this is not the seasonal movement of the herders," said Daniel Gelan, professor and pastoralist development researcher with Vision University in Ethiopia. "They are leaving the northern region for good because they can no longer sustain camel herding there because of extreme heat and drought," he told the BBC. The Horn of Africa has been recovering from the longest and most severe recorded drought spanning from 2020 to 2023, but now it is seeing another round of the harsh condition, according to charity organisation Oxfam. It said water prices in some parts of Somalia had risen by more than 2,000% because of the drought. Umer, the cattle herder in Afar, said he was worried that an outbreak of disease could start killing his adult camels anytime now. His concern is valid, according to Prof Chehma. "While camels remain the livestock herders' last line of defence against the desert, the unprecedented intensity of current heatwaves directly threatens their vital functions, turning this historically resilient animal into a climate victim," he said. Additional reporting by BBC Amharic's Firehiwot Kassa You may also be interested in: - One boy's fight to save his family from starvation - Published15 November 2022
- Camel milk: Kenya's new 'white gold' Video, 00:01:43 - Published2 February 2021
- The women saving Somalia's animals. Video, 00:01:24 - Published24 May 2017
Go to BBCAfrica.com, external for more news from the African continent. Follow us on Twitter @BBCAfrica, external, on Facebook at BBC Africa, external or on Instagram at bbcafrica, external BBC Africa podcasts Sign up for our Future Earth newsletter to keep up with the latest climate and environment stories with the BBC's Justin Rowlatt. Outside the UK? Sign up to our international newsletter here.

item.title: Blocked by censors, China's animal lovers take fight against abuse offline and overseas
item.description:
【原文链接】：https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/cqx7wd3x420o?at_medium=RSS&at_campaign=rss
========================================
Blocked by censors, China's animal lovers take fight against abuse offline and overseas - Published When videos showing the horrific killing of a stray dog and her puppies sparked outrage on the Chinese internet in June, Beijing's censorship machine kicked into action. But what was unusual this time was that it pitted the shocked and angry voices against authorities who said they were concerned about the four boys involved, all of them younger than 14. China's Communist Party regime tightly controls media and the internet to suppress criticism of the state and to manage public anger. But in this case authorities cited the age of the boys as they urged people to withhold their comments and scrubbed social media posts about the incident. "We urge the public not to disseminate related information, to reject cyberbullying, and to work together to create a good environment for the growth of minors," reads an official statement. But the restrictions only seemed to spur the outcry. China's animal lovers have now taken their fight offline and abroad, even putting up billboards in New York to drum up support. In China, posts about the incident are still drawing thousands of comments across social media, three weeks after the incident. There is shock, anger and sorrow, as well as demands for laws against animal cruelty, which China currently does not have. Disturbing footage showing the boys in the city of Jieyang, Guangdong province, hitting three puppies with sticks before setting their mother on fire emerged online at the end of June. The boys were seen laughing as the dog, named Wang Wang, burned. Jieyang authorities said last month that the boys would not face criminal charges and would instead be sent to a "specialised school", but did not elaborate. Some reports suggest the boys filmed themselves and uploaded the video online, but police have not commented on that. Censors have tried to block the video to protect the boys' identity, although some people argue that it should stay. Their identities have not been revealed, but snippets of the video with their faces visible continue to circulate online. There are also unverified posts claiming to be close-ups of the boys' faces, raising the risk of other children being wrongly identified. Countries around the world restrict information about child offenders or set a high bar for identifying them. For instance, media coverage and public access to juvenile court documents are often restricted to minimise psychological harm caused by undue publicity. In this case, Chinese authorities have not completely banned the discussion yet. Keywords such as "Wang Wang" and "animal protection" came up as trending search terms on Monday, but posts related to them started to disappear towards the end of this week. Some users have also reported that their ability to share others' posts has been restricted. It is hard to say if the deleted or restricted content had named or identified any of the four boys. Officials have not said anything more on the matter. Posts by several celebrities, including Chinese actor Du Jiang and Taiwanese actress Joe Chen, who had called for China to strengthen protection for animals also disappeared. But it's unclear if these were taken down by the celebrities themselves. Chinese users have refused to back down though, with some turning to advocates abroad to air Wang Wang's plight. It is hard to say why this, of all the sensitive issues that are censored in China, has galvanised such pushback. Part of the reason could be that it's the latest in a string of animal abuse cases that have made headlines recently. And the footage seems to have had a visceral impact. Animal lover PL tells BBC Chinese she found it "affecting in a particularly severe way". "We can't make our voices heard, so many people [outside China] are actively helping us to share this message," the woman in eastern China says. The International Organisation for Animal Protection based in Italy says it has received more than 100 reports from people across the world, calling for the organisation to intervene. It added that it had sought more information from Chinese authorities. In New York City's Times Square, one of the busiest pedestrian intersections in the world, photographs of Wang Wang come up on a gigantic digital screen every 35 minutes. One of the captions reads: "She was loved. She wanted to live. She should still be here." Similar billboards have been put up on a building near Taipei 101, an iconic 101-floor skyscraper in Taiwan. This one includes a picture of Wang Wang nursing her puppies. "This planet is their home too. Justice for Wang Wang," the caption reads. CK, a Taiwanese woman who spent NT$30,000 ($930; £692) putting up the billboard, says she hopes it can foster "a sense of unity". "It makes everyone feel like we're speaking out together on this issue, rather than airing our individual opinions online," she tells BBC Chinese. CK has also set up accounts on platforms like Reddit and X to share the animals' plight. "The world need to know about the cruelty these dogs have been subject to," CK says, adding that she couldn't sleep or eat properly for days after seeing a clip of the brutal killing. Elara, another Taiwanese woman, used AI to translate a summary of the incident into more than 10 languages, such as Japanese, Portuguese and Russian, which have been circulated online. Residents in Hong Kong and Macau have raised funds to put up posters on buses and trains. Within mainland China, the debate continues, with much of the conversation centred around creating awareness to demand laws against animal cruelty. A striking cartoon of Wang Wang and her puppies caught the eye of many football fans during a domestic game at a stadium in Dalian on Saturday. "Every life deserves respect and kindness," read the cartoon that was displayed on a huge screen before the game. Homegrown businesses, such as beauty brand Amortals and streetwear label Wah Yan Ching Nin, have also placed ads outside shopping malls across the country, calling for an end to animal abuse. Stickers and car decals featuring Wang Wang and her puppies have also been put up for sale on China's largest e-commerce platform Taobao. Some of these efforts have met roadblocks. Wan Jia Li, a mall in the city of Changsha, was reportedly instructed to remove billboards featuring Wang Wang. "We can't do animal-protection billboards anymore... My boss told me so. I'm not sure if the notice came from the government, or why we have been asked to take them down," an employee in the mall's management team told the BBC. Still, people have continued to rally online. "Let's keep posting. Don't let the heat of discussion cool down," reads a comment on Weibo. "Constantly speaking up does not mean disturbing social order. [We're doing this] to let more people see it and realise it's such an important thing that we do not mistreat animals," reads another.

item.title: Liquor store employee narrowly escapes leopard attack
item.description:
【原文链接】：https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/videos/cly893zrjgdo?at_medium=RSS&at_campaign=rss
========================================
Liquor store employee narrowly escapes leopard attack CCTV footage showed the moment a leopard entered a liquor store and attacked a salesman in Rajasthan, north western India. Earlier, the animal had already attacked a cleaning worker inside. Afterwards, the leopard hid near the counter so both employees managed to escape. Others helped shut the shop, trapping the leopard inside. After nearly five hours, officials tranquillised and captured the animal. It was later released back into the wild, local authorities confirmed. Reporting by Mohar Singh Meena and Purushottam Joshi of BBC News Hindi Related topics - IndiaUpdates from your News topics will appear in My News and in a collection on the News homepage. - LeopardsUpdates from your News topics will appear in My News and in a collection on the News homepage.

item.title: Watch: Louvre reopens gallery after jewellery heist
item.description:
【原文链接】：https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/videos/c1m15l8kgejo?at_medium=RSS&at_campaign=rss
========================================
Watch: Louvre reopens gallery after jewellery heist The Louvre Museum in Paris reopened its Galerie d'Apollon (Gallery of Apollo) to visitors on Wednesday, nine months after jewellery worth millions was stolen in a brazen heist. But the gallery, which once housed France's crown jewels, reopened without any on display. "This is an iconic space within the Louvre, but it will no longer house the Crown Jewels and gemstones, which are to be displayed in secure areas elsewhere in the museum", said the museum's director, Christophe Leribault. The heist took place on 19 October when masked thieves used a truck with a set of mechanical ladders mounted on it to gain access to the gallery via a balcony. Police have arrested four people who prosecutors allege are the thieves - but the stolen items have still not been recovered. Related topics - LouvreUpdates from your News topics will appear in My News and in a collection on the News homepage. - ParisUpdates from your News topics will appear in My News and in a collection on the News homepage. - FranceUpdates from your News topics will appear in My News and in a collection on the News homepage. - EuropeUpdates from your News topics will appear in My News and in a collection on the News homepage.

item.title: Watch: Beluga whales travel by plane and lorry to US from closed Canadian marine park
item.description:
【原文链接】：https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/videos/ckg4x8lpe50o?at_medium=RSS&at_campaign=rss
========================================
Watch: Beluga whales travel by plane and lorry to US from closed Canadian marine park Six beluga whales have arrived at aquariums in the United States after being moved from a closed Canadian marine park that could no longer care for the mammals. They are part of a large pod of 30 belugas living at Marineland of Canada that need to be rehomed. Sierra, Acadia, Osiris and Lillooet arrived at a Chicago facility, while Bertie Botts and Frankie arrived at SeaWorld in San Antonio, Texas. Transported in specialised cold-water containers by truck and plane, the animals were accompanied throughout the journey by marine mammal veterinarians. This is the first stage of an unprecedented international relocation effort. The move followed months of planning and veterinary checks, with specialist teams now monitoring the whales as they settle into their new homes. Related topics - United StatesUpdates from your News topics will appear in My News and in a collection on the News homepage. - CanadaUpdates from your News topics will appear in My News and in a collection on the News homepage. - US & CanadaUpdates from your News topics will appear in My News and in a collection on the News homepage. - AnimalsUpdates from your News topics will appear in My News and in a collection on the News homepage. - WhalesUpdates from your News topics will appear in My News and in a collection on the News homepage.
